data.title: Fed's Interest Rate Decision: April 29, 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Federal Reserve concluded its third meeting of the year by maintaining the federal funds rate at 3.50%–3.75%. The decision, which markets had fully priced in, keeps the benchmark rate at its lowest level since November 2022 for the third meeting in a row.

Here is a statement from the meeting:

Recent indicators suggest that economic activity has been expanding at a solid pace. Job gains have remained low, on average, and the unemployment rate has been little changed in recent months. Inflation is elevated, in part reflecting the recent increase in global energy prices.

The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. Developments in the Middle East are contributing to a high level of uncertainty about the economic outlook. The Committee is attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate.

In support of its goals, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 3‑1/2 to 3‑3/4 percent. In considering the extent and timing of additional adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks. The Committee is strongly committed to supporting maximum employment and returning inflation to its 2 percent objective.

In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.

Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Michael S. Barr; Michelle W. Bowman; Lisa D. Cook; Philip N. Jefferson; Anna Paulson; and Christopher J. Waller. Voting against this action were Stephen I. Miran, who preferred to lower the target range for the federal funds rate by 1/4 percentage point at this meeting; and Beth M. Hammack, Neel Kashkari, and Lorie K. Logan, who supported maintaining the target range for the federal funds rate but did not support inclusion of an easing bias in the statement at this time.

Background on the Federal Funds Rate (FFR)

The federal funds rate is the interest rate that banks charge each other to borrow money overnight. It is set by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), a committee within the Federal Reserve, which meets eight times a year. It is a primary tool used by the Federal Reserve to implement monetary policy and is a key driver of economic activity.

While it directly affects short-term borrowing between banks, the effects of the FFR can be felt across a variety of entities. For consumers, changes in the FFR influence mortgage rates, credit card interest, auto loans, and savings yields. For businesses, it affects borrowing costs and investment decisions. Additionally, financial markets also react to rate changes, with shifts in bond yields and equity performance.

The FOMC adjusts interest rates based on key economic indicators, focusing on inflation, employment, economic growth, and income. The Fed has a dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment.

It's easy to see in the chart above how the FOMC's comments from their press release are ringing true. The unemployment rate has moved between 4.3%-4.5% over the past nine months. Meanwhile, inflation has proven sticky over the past few years, with the most recent figures for both the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and the PCE Price Index still well above the Fed's 2% target level.

Historical Trends of the Federal Funds Rate (FFR)

The stagflation crisis of the late 1970s and early 1980s demanded drastic measures. Under the leadership of Paul Volcker, the Federal Reserve pushed the FFR to a historic high of 20.06% in January 1981. This aggressive tightening of monetary policy was instrumental in curbing runaway inflation, albeit at the cost of a significant economic slowdown.

In stark contrast, the FFR was driven to near-zero levels in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis and again during the economic turmoil of the 2020 pandemic. Specifically, the FFR reached a record low of approximately 0.04% in May 2020. These periods of ultra-low interest rates aimed to stimulate borrowing, investment, and economic recovery.

The federal funds rate has undergone significant fluctuations in the past two decades. Following the 2008 financial crisis, the Fed kept rates near zero until 2016. A gradual tightening cycle brought the rate to 2.25%-2.50% by 2019, but the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic led to a return to near-zero rates in 2020.

In response to soaring inflation - the highest in four decades - the Fed aggressively raised rates from March 2022 to August 2023, reaching a peak of 5.25%-5.50%, the highest level since early 2001. The central bank then shifted course in September 2024, implementing three consecutive rate cuts to bring the FFR to the range of 4.25%-4.50%.

The Fed went on a similar path in 2025, holding rates steady until September, where they then issued three consecutive cuts to bring the FFR to its current range of 3.50%-3.75%.

Federal Funds Rate: What's Next?

The CME FedWatch Tool estimates the probability of future interest rate moves. The tool is updated in real-time in response to economic data releases, Fed statements, and market movements. The chart below shows the tool’s predictions through the end of 2027. (Note: The chart below is at the time of writing, and expectations may have shifted since publication.)

The market is currently pricing in zero rate movements for 2026 and one 25 basis point cut in December 2027.

Once a quarter the Fed releases their "dot plot" to reveal each FOMC member's individual projection for the target level of the federal funds rate (FFR) at the end of the current year, at the end of the next two years, and in the "longer run".

The latest "dot plot" from March 2026 reveals a committee that has grown more unified in the near term while simultaneously raising its expectations for where rates will eventually settle. Most participants now anticipate the federal funds rate will hold between 3.25% and 3.75% through the end of 2026, showing a much tighter consensus than previous projections.

However, this clarity dissolves in the later years, with 2027 and 2028 showing a wide divergence of opinions ranging from 2.375% to nearly 4.0%. Notably, the "longer run" anchor has shifted upward to 3.0%, signaling a collective belief that the neutral rate is higher than previously estimated.

The Fed’s next meeting is scheduled for June 16-17, where the next "dot plot" will also be released.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alphabet: The Stock Actually Became Cheaper After Q1
data.description:
A few days ago, I wrote up Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOGL, GOOG) earnings preview. A preview where my tone was between neutral and bullish. On one hand, I believed in a strong earnings release that would justify the $4 trillion market cap. On the other hand, I expected volatility, mainly with the market being a bit more cautious about the size of CapEx, etc.

And even with these expectations I had, I was surprised. The GOOG Q1 earnings were even better than I expected, and the market was not cautious about CapEx. Proof of this is that in the after-hours GOOGL stock is up 6.5%, due to the strong positive surprises in Search and Cloud and also the double beat.

Alphabet Q1 Earnings: Very Strong

I wrote this in the earnings preview:

“Particularly, I think what would be most interesting as a positive surprise is Search. If it maintains solid growth or even accelerates slightly, proving that “AI mode” and new Google features are increasing engagement and consequently revenue, that would be very bullish for the stock.”

Now, Alphabet just reported $60.4 billion in revenue in “Google Search & Other,” a growth of 19% YoY. Compared to the growth closer to the low teens that Alphabet usually delivers, this growth is exceptional. But more than that, it weakens the bear thesis that the segment is being cannibalized by LLMs and/or is losing strength. In fact, it is gaining strength. The CEO, Sundar Pichai, explained that “AI experiences” are driving usage and queries to an all-time high. This, for me, is the pillar of Alphabet’s bullish case.

The subscriptions segment also performed very well, with 19% YoY growth, also driven by strong YouTube and Google One. In addition, it was a good period for Gemini AI plans. Network revenue continued declining at around 4%, which was already expected, and YouTube Ads grew 11%, which seems solid to me.

Also, Google Cloud division was exceptionally strong. The numbers speak for themselves. The backlog doubled QoQ, reaching more than $460 billion. YoY revenue growth was 63%.

Now, I would like you to take a quick look at my previous assumptions for Alphabet in 2026, where I tried to be more conservative. The assumption for search growth was only 10%; after Q1, I need to revise that upward. YouTube Ads and Network were in line/slightly below. Subscriptions were slightly above. Cloud: I already estimated strong growth for the year: 45%, but with the new backlog and growth above 60%, I will also have to revise upward.

Regarding margins, the company also delivered solid and healthy numbers, an operating margin of 36%, driven both by a consistent improvement in services and also a substantial YoY improvement in Cloud.

I am saying this because all analysts will have to make revisions to Alphabet’s top line, and this has a powerful effect on the stock and a very positive effect on long-term shareholder value expectations.

Alphabet Long-Term Thesis: Another “Everything” Company

“The” everything company is Amazon (AMZN), right? Strong e-commerce, leader in cloud, initiatives in communications, streaming, and so on. But increasingly it seems to me that Alphabet is going down this path and making its ecosystem not only better and more connected but also broader.

We have the ads segment, with Google Search, Google Maps, YouTube Ads, and others. For subscriptions, there is YouTube subscription, Gemini, Google One, and even Google Play. In the middle of all this, Alphabet also has Android.

As I mentioned in the section above, Alphabet also showed efficiency in Cloud; of course, it was already large, but with more than $6.5 billion in operating income in a single quarter (and more than $20 billion in revenue), Alphabet showed that it is large, in excellent momentum, and reached a profitable level. It is worth mentioning that tokens per minute already exceed 16 billion (with a 60% QoQ growth), showing that Alphabet is also an excellent AI company and is in this race to remain among the most important in the segment.

But the icing on the cake, in my view, is Alphabet's Waymo, which reached 500 thousand autonomous rides per week. Recently I covered Tesla’s (TSLA) results and liked the progress they made (check it here). But Alphabet’s impresses me even more. Waymo is already in a mature stage, and the most impressive thing is that few people factor this into a bull case; that is, it is hard to see someone invested in Alphabet because eventually it can unlock a very large total addressable market in autonomous vehicles.

Just to mention a few more parts of the ecosystem, Alphabet also has investments in SpaceX (SPACE) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) (check it here). Speaking of this type of investment, in Q1 the “Gain on equity securities” was $36.9 billion.

This seems like the perfect scenario for the bull case. The core is doing very well. Now there is more conviction that Search will not be disrupted by LLMs and, in fact, can continue positively impacting the mix and top-line growth. Even so, the other “complementary” parts are also delivering great results, like YouTube. Together with Cloud, this would already be more than enough diversification, but with optionalities the game becomes even more interesting, because these are things that in a few years can unlock value that was not even that much on the radar.

Alphabet’s Valuation: Now It's Cheaper

Alphabet stock just rose more than 6% in the after-hours. However, with Q1 earnings, the stock became more undervalued. This is because even though the market cap increased, the financials and the expectations improved at a faster pace.

As I mentioned in the first section, I had to make some revisions to my assumptions. As you can see in the table, I slightly improved the assumptions for the growth of the Search & Other division, mainly for 2026, but now maintaining at a more solid level for the following years, due to the positive signals from Q1. For Cloud, I also significantly increased the expected growth for the coming years. And finally, for operating margin, I slightly increased the expansion pace, reaching 36% in 2030.

These are still very reasonable assumptions, but the multiples are already attractive. Now we are talking about a price-to-operating income of 23x for 2027, slightly lower than before (when the market cap was smaller). In the long term this effect is even better perceived. Before, the estimates were an operating income of $234 billion with a P/OI of 17.4x (2030). Now we already see an operating income of almost $290 billion and a P/OI of ~15.5x.

The Verdict

I still have some concern about GOOGL’s valuation. The P/E for 2026 is close to 29x, and for 2027 it drops to 25x. As the last 5-year average points to a forward P/E of 23x, the margin of safety is not that large.

But with such a strong result, it is hard not to be optimistic about Alphabet. Cloud growth, in my view, only shows that all this infrastructure buildout (high CapEx) is being utilized and will make growth even more accelerated, not to mention the strong Search momentum. Therefore, even with a valuation above average, it seems reasonable to maintain a solid buy rating for GOOGL stock.

data.title: Hexcel Corporation: Strong Momentum And Earnings Growth Support Buy Rating
data.description:
Previous Coverage

In early November last year I reported on Hexcel Corporation (HXL) for the first time and rated the stock a BUY based on improving fundamentals, a favorable valuation, and an appealing potential forward return supported by strong projected earnings growth. Since my initial coverage, the stock has fluctuated some, peaking at more than $95 per share and exceeding a 30% return in early March. However, following that apex, the stock dwindled lower before recovering some over the last month. Over the past ~6 months, HXL has vastly outpaced the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), returning more than 6 times the ETF.

Earlier this week, Hexcel announced their Q1 2026 earnings, so let's dig into the call and assess how the company has performed, as well as where it might go, and also look at an updated valuation based on refreshed free cash flow numbers.

Q1 2026 Earnings And Looking Ahead

As noted above, on Wednesday, April 22nd, the company announced their most recent earnings report, where it posted Non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.59, exceeding the consensus by $0.15, on revenue slightly above $500 million, which beat analyst expectations by nearly $18 million and was a year-over-year increase of almost 10%.

Starting with the Composite Materials segment, sales increased from $365.3 million in Q1 2025 to $398.8 million in the most recent quarter, an increase of $33.5 million, or a little more than 9%. Within this segment, Commercial Aerospace saw an enormous jump in revenue of approximately $40 million, or more than 16%. These strong results were driven by increased volumes for both Airbus and Boeing programs and reinforced by stabilizing inventory levels, heightened OEM build rates, and overall aerospace recovery. Moving on to the Defense, Space & Other piece within Composite Materials, sales declined about $6 million, or 4.8%, partially because of the divestiture of an Austrian industrial business, as well as weaker volumes within the Space business unit, particularly for rocket motors. Conversely, Defense earned modest organic growth thanks to strong demand for fighter aircraft in Europe and continued demand for military helicopters, both stateside and in Europe.

The other segment, Engineered products, posted double-digit growth of 12.6%, as total Q1 2026 revenue was $11.5 million higher relative to Q1 2025, coming in at almost $103 million. Commercial Aerospace, under this segment, had revenue climb significantly, from $38.3 million to $51.5 million, a jump of more than 34% for the same reasons listed above: robust demand within the Airbus and Boeing programs. The Defense, Space & Other portion of the business here incurred a small decline in sales, dipping a little more than 3% year-over-year.

Additionally, management reaffirmed its previously issued guidance for fiscal 2026 sales of $2.05 billion at the midpoint, which equates to potential YoY revenue growth of about 8.5%. While Non-GAAP earnings per share are estimated to be $2.20 at the midpoint.

The day after the earnings release, which can be read or listened to here, shares of Hexcel climbed around 5% but gave a few percentage points back last Friday.

Looking ahead to the next earnings call, analysts are projecting earnings per share to be around $0.59, with revenue coming in at $525 million. It's worth noting that, of the analysts covering the company, three revised their estimates higher during the last 90 days, while 5 have reduced their forecasts.

Bull And Bear Cases

Below are a couple of growth catalysts that might support a bull case.

  • As indicated on slide 5 of the recent quarterly investor presentation, composites are making up a more significant portion of each new generation of aircraft, driven by the need for a lighter plane and greater fuel efficiency. Composites are Hexcel's main product, so the company stands to benefit greatly from this movement, especially since many new aircraft are composed of at least 50% composite materials and the proportion of composite materials in new planes doubles approximately every five years.
  • Many of Hexcel's contracts with original equipment manufacturers, like Airbus & Boeing, are long-term agreements and run for multiple years and can make it difficult for their customers to find another reliable supplier that can meet their demand. Products must meet stringent qualifications and go through extensive testing in order to obtain clearance from the FAA. This leads to high switching costs for their customers and is ultimately a benefit for Hexcel. To add a little icing on the cake, these contracts also typically include escalation and/or pass-through clauses, which result in higher raw material costs being passed on to customers, removing some of that burden from HXL.

Below are some risks/headwinds that could impact future results.

  • Potential supply chain issues related to the raw materials in Hexcel's products could lead to uncertainty for them and their customers. The tariffs definitely caused a problem for the business last year, but that appears to have subsided for the time being. Also, in an earnings call last year, management did state they source their raw materials from more than a handful of countries, which is obviously good business, but any sudden shock to their supply chain or obtaining materials could lead to a bottleneck in production.
  • Hexcel reduced capital spending during the pandemic due to the uncertainty surrounding the situation, which at that time was the right thing to do in order to preserve liquidity. This mostly helped the business as aircraft demand suffered and inventories built up, but over time this has led to capacity constraints, especially since Airbus and Boeing have been increasing production rates. The company's CapEx spend has been higher since 2020 but is still playing a little catch-up.

Hexcel Valuation And Return Potential

The last piece of this puzzle is to determine if HXL is trading for an appealing valuation, and to do so, we'll look at it from a free cash flow perspective.

I estimate a stock's implied fair value based on its free cash flow yield, and specifically how the current yield compares to its historical average. This helps me determine how much investors have typically been willing to pay for a company's free cash flow and how attractive the stock looks today relative to that history. The analysis is calculated on 12 years of daily historical price data, capturing the rolling 5-year average free cash flow yield for the past 7 years. The valuation chart below juxtaposes HXL's actual share price and the fair value range calculated using this methodology.

The key assumption behind this valuation model is that investors have paid a fair price for the stock over the past 5 years, on average. However, since every valuation is an estimate, there is a +/-10% margin of error applied to the fair value range.

My custom free cash flow valuation model indicates Hexcel is trading for close to its fair value, with a current price of ~$91.50 and a fair price of $94. This is quite the change from my last coverage, where the actual price was $72 and the estimated value was $79. The new figures point to the potential fair value rising close to 20% over the last six months, despite a small decline in the FCF per share for the TTM relative to the FCF per share in FY 2025.

The 3-5 year expected rate of return for Hexcel comes in at a robust 29.36%, with the bulk of that estimate stemming from projected future earnings growth of more than 28%, while the forward dividend yield adds another 0.77%, and the return to fair value factor increases it just 0.20%.

Final Thoughts

Hexcel has posted strong gains since late last year and appears to still be trading for close to its fair value. The recent earnings report showed some mixed signals, but overall it should be viewed positively, with demand rising. Conversely, the company could run into some issues should they face higher input costs they are unable to pass along to their customers or run into any hiccups following the ramping up of production for Airbus and Boeing. Despite these risks, the positives definitely outweigh the negatives for me, and as such, I rate the stock a BUY even with the stock trading at or near full valuation, as the momentum and earnings growth should support continued price appreciation.

data.title: Mercedes-Benz: Automotive Upside Is Clearer At This Point In 2026
data.description:
It's fair to say that the automotive sector isn't loved right now (most manufacturers), and it's also fair to say that Mercedes (MBGYY) is a company that is, in fact, not loved. While my position over time is still not deeply negative due to a very low cost basis, the recent few months of performance have been poor, to say the least. The company is being viewed as a "HOLD" by most - and even a "SELL" by Wall Street analyst averages.

So what do I see that causes me to, at least provisionally, hold a different view on the company here? My latest article is about 4 months old at this point, and the results posted today warrant an update for the company valuation target and my estimates.

I not only invest in automotive manufacturers, I also invest in the entire space at the right valuation. This includes parts and seating, like Magna (MGA), it includes airbags like Autoliv (ALV), it includes technology and chips like Infineon (IFNNY), and it also includes the straight manufacturers, like Mercedes-Benz. Infineon is a good example of an automotive investment that has in fact paid excellent dividends (in the sense that my returns for the company have been superb from my point of investment). Oftentimes, when you invest in a company at a low, and set a target (and miss out on that target), you fail to see the objective upside beyond the current market sentiment.

It's currently my view that Mercedes-Benz is a tricky company to invest in. I've honestly made more money with Daimler Trucks (DTRUY) the past few years. But I do believe that the company's "day will come".

Please also note that in my last article, I made a case for rotation, which I also did at profit for 65% of my stake. This does not mean that my current position is "scot-free" or that I defend myself or the performance using this. It highlights certain aspects of my strategy, however, that I believe to be beneficial

I'll try to show you why here - what makes, contrary to what seems to be the general sentiment here, Mercedes a decent investment, if you adjust your expectations accordingly?

Mercedes-Benz - What works and what doesn't in 2026

Despite the reaction we've seen in the past few months, including the up and downs in March - April of this year, the company has absolutely no issues with either profitability or being at an attractive valuation - something which also the Quant factoring/ranking on SA correctly (in my view) sees.

Mercedes currently trades at a native MBG ticker price of below €50/share. The only way that this can be considered close to unattractive is if you either estimate the company's growth as being incorrect - it's currently estimated at 16% per year on average until 2028E - or you don't believe one of the most luxurious automotive companies on the planet with an A rating in credit and a yield of over 6% that's well-covered should be trading at around 10x P/E with that growth rate estimate.

I view both of the affirmative answers to those assumptions as being wrong. That is to say, I believe growth is there, and I believe the company should be traded at least at 10x P/E.

Earnings were released today, and I don't quite get some of the negativity that I am seeing (more on that later). My main complaint about Mercedes has been the commoditization of the brand, and that it needs to move back to its luxury roots, rather than seeking to please everyone. It seems that Mercedes is doing that. The company's high-margin sales of the G-class, S-class, Maybach models and the new S-class were integral to the company's maintaining of its margin and profit, despite declining volumes. This is, what I think, Mercedes should be. Expertly-engineered automobiles that sell in lower volumes, but at high margins.

The company managed close to €2B worth of FCF in the first quarter, and net liquidity was at €33.8B, which was superb, if you consider what the company is actually going through right now in terms of EVs and the like.

If you follow my work, you know that I am dubious of the appeal of EV's and BEV's in general. This is why I view the sales growth of EU BEV models with a bit of a shrug - it's good, but I personally don't believe this to drive sales permanently. This is why I welcomed the company's backtrack to diesel and gas cars, and I'm pleased to see the company working on its strategy that we've seen since February of last year.

While I believe EV's to have applications, I don't think it's everyone - far from it. Electric car demand in much of Europe, especially southern and eastern, has failed. If you visit any area in South America or Africa, you'll find EV's to be a minority or non-existent, and the fact that the company makes very little profit on them to begin with doesn't exactly speak in favor of them. Even the new S-class is combustion-powered.

For the company, the combination of successful Van numbers, the excellent cost control despite margin pressure and the solid underlying growth in virtually every relevant market brought a good set of overall returns. What is to be remembered here above all is that this is a ramp up phase for the company, not the end-all, be-all.

It's what makes it so encouraging to see such solid returns for the company's various segments, doing well despite significant headwinds which the company can do very little about. The cash generation was superb, despite a massive restructuring payment of close to €1B.

For 2026, sales revenue is going to be the same level as YoY (estimate), while EBIT is likely to improve very significantly. Anything positive in the EV segment for the company is just a bonus for me.

To me, as an analyst following MB, the stock and the company is moving more or less as I expected it to in terms of results during a transitional period. That the market views this as a negative is not something I can control beyond how I determine to act as a result of it - and my reaction is to update my valuation and targets.

Mercedes-Benz - Upside with 15%+ annualized seems clear even at just 8-9x P/E

I mentioned that I view the company as fairly valued at a 10x P/E, but MB does not in fact need this to outperform. Even forecasting at 8-9x P/E, the annualized rates of return exceed 24% per year, and at a 20-year average, the company is cited to breach 27-29% per year if these growth estimates materialize.

In my latest article in January, I gave the company a €78/share PT. I'm maintaining this target for the long term, with the emphasis that it is the long term. Remember that I in fact spoke about rotating in my last article, which saw MB trade at less than €64/share. But again, this one is a longer-term estimate.

The only way for MB not to hit my targets and estimates eventually would be either a failure to grow, or a failure to be traded above 7x P/E, which is the current "cut off" for that 15% annualized per year.

So let's review what it would actually take for Mercedes to not grow going forward until 2028-2029, despite restructuring, investments, and positive demand.

A sustained downturn in China's EV is a core part of this thesis, but it's something I have already somewhat accounted for in my estimates. Sales declines are things I've modelled for, and I frankly don't believe the Chinese market is going to be a major driver for the company. That's also why I value closer to 7-8x P/E in terms of PT, and not 10x. It's already something I include and account for.

Secondly, it would be based on the recent sales results, which require a complete failure of its "luxury first" strategy. Because Mercedes entire model hinges on being able to charge a premium for premium vehicles, the failure to do so would mean the collapse of growth. Affluent customers shifting to cheaper models would impact this. But as of right now, there are very few signs that this is what is happening to Mercedes.

Third, the perhaps most realistic risk is structural issues to its bottom line in the form of tariffs and geopolitical issues that Mercedes can do absolutely nothing about. The persistent high costs faced by Mercedes if combined with long-term geopolitical conflicts, could prevent further margin expansion, and this could also influence EPS growth, or lack thereof.

The perfect storm of these factors in the long term is what I in the end consider to be the less likely scenario here. Stagnation until 2028 is materially unlikely due to its ongoing product offensive, which is showing results, its cost efficiency program (again, showing results), good cash flow generation despite material and deep challenges, which showcases the strength of the brand and the company, and its openness to switching back to ICE as well. The 2026E outlook represents a material improvement to the 2025 results. I therefore view it as a very negative perspective to say that 2026 isn't going to be significantly better than YoY, and thus I maintain my PT of €78/share as of this time, for this A-rated automaker.

As of the recent dip, I may in fact add more in the weakness here.

Risks To My Thesis

Aside from the aforementioned risk scenario, pricing power and the core of its new strategy are the two primary risks to the thesis here. Mercedes is facing competition from many new entrants (Chinese) who seek to offer "luxury at affordable prices" or, at the very least, a better value proposition. However, those who argue forget one of the core tenets of luxury—that when you commoditize luxury, it is no longer luxury. I believe it's actually one of the reasons BMW and Mercedes "failed" a bit in forgetting or ignoring this for too long. I have covered this risk before, and I continue to view this as very core to an understanding of Mercedes and of luxury.

I continue to view the company's market share as lost and unable to recover in China. It's my view that Mercedes should significantly downscale and focus only on the super-high-end in that market. Unfortunately, the company's late arrival to BEV/EV means that CapEx will remain more elevated for longer than its competition as well.

For these reasons, I expect the company to remain quite volatile over time, and the risks to be relatively complex. I confirm this volatility as of this quarter and article. I'm willing to go in again for a similar, positive RoR as last time, and give the company the following thesis and targets.

Thesis

Mercedes is one of the most qualitative and premium car companies out there. It owns some of the most desirable cars and brands on the planet, and despite some of its recent pushes, in my view, going in the wrong direction, I believe at heart the company remains a qualitative automotive business with a lot of forward potential—this remains after 1Q26.

In most forward scenarios, I still view Mercedes-Benz as undervalued here, and that is why I give the company a "Buy" rating and consider it cheap. Out of all the automakers in Germany, this one is one of the more attractive ones, and I am planning to expand my position in it going forward. I maintain my €78/share PT.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €78/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta Q1 Review: The Market Has Lost Its Mind
data.description:
Article Thesis

Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) reported very strong Q1 earnings results on Wednesday, beating estimates on both lines once again. And yet, the market sold off META's shares -- despite the fact that the company was already pretty cheap going into the earnings date. To me, this makes no sense at all, which is why I think this is a big overreaction and that investors are getting a nice buying opportunity right now.

Past Coverage

I have written about Meta Platforms here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently around three months ago, when I published this article. I was bullish on META back then, which worked out reasonably well, as META's shares are up slightly more than the broad market since that article was published. With META reporting its Q1 results on Wednesday, I want to update my views on the company today.

What Happened?

On Wednesday, following the market's close, Meta Platforms released its fiscal Q1 results, which included the following headline numbers:

We see green, which is good news -- META beat the consensus estimate on both lines, outperforming the Wall Street revenue estimate by around 1.5% and delivering a huge earnings per share beat, with the bottom line number coming in more than 55% ahead of what was expected by the analyst community. And yet, shares didn't jump up following this earnings release -- they actually declined. At the time of writing, META is down 7%, a move that destroyed around $120 billion in market capitalization. As I'll explain in this article, that doesn't make sense to me, as META's report looks pretty good overall.

Meta Platforms' Q1 Performance: Pretty Good

Let's delve into META's results. Starting with the user count, we see the first good news -- growth continued, despite the already extremely huge number of users. Across META's different platforms, it had 3.56 billion daily active users, up 4%, or a little more than 100 million, versus one year earlier. While a 4% growth rate doesn't seem overly much at first sight, this is just one of META's growth drivers -- and adding 100 million new users in a single year is a very impressive feat for any company. I like that META keeps growing its user count, as it is, I believe, a good sign that its services and networks remain highly relevant for people around the world -- otherwise, more people would be leaving or would at least stop being active so often. But while the growing user count is a positive sign, it's not the most relevant growth driver -- a growing revenue per user number contributes more to META's top-line growth rate. The following slide from META's earnings presentation shows the impact very well:

Meta Platforms' revenue per user grew from $12.36 one year ago to $15.66 as of the most recent quarter, which makes for a growth rate of around 27%. That's significantly better than the average revenue per user growth rate one year earlier, as this metric improved by 10% between Q1 2024 and Q1 2025. That was still a very solid result, but the increase over the last year was way more powerful. I think this means that META's pricing power has improved, with advertisers being willing to pay more and more for META's advertising space, while more ad impressions per user also impacted the average revenue per user metric positively.

So we can summarize that many things are going in META's favor: the company has more users, who are seeing more ads, and Meta Platforms gets more money per ad. With all these variables moving in META's favor, the company was able to grow its revenues by more than 30% compared to one year earlier -- one of the best growth rates among the Magnificent 7 stocks, with Nvidia (NVDA) being the only one that is showing an even better growth rate compared to META.

Revenue growth is good, but investors naturally want earnings growth, too. Here, META did quite well, with its operating margin remaining stable versus one year earlier, at 41%. Ideally, the company's operating margin would expand as it scales up and benefits from operating leverage, but due to META's heavy Artificial Intelligence investments, that's not the case right now. META is spending a lot on AI talent and buys new chips at a rapid pace, with those capital expenditures resulting in higher depreciation charges. If management is right, those investments will pay off handsomely in the future -- the fact that META's operating margin is "only" remaining stable in the 40s instead of rising further is a small price to pay, I think. Other tech companies are seeing margin pressure due to their AI investments, so META looks comparatively good with its very strong and stable operating margin of more than 40%.

Thanks to META's strong revenue growth, the company was able to grow its profits nicely, with operating profit climbing from $17.6 billion to $22.9 billion over the last year. This was a little less than one quarter earlier, but META always experienced a bit of a sequential decline between Q4 and Q1, due to Q4 always being especially strong thanks to the holiday effect -- consumer goods companies spend a lot on advertisements in Q4.

META's net earnings and earnings per share soared by more than 60% on a GAAP basis, but that was partially due to tax effects, with META seeing an income tax benefit during Q1. This is not typical, which is why the GAAP net profit growth rate is somewhat distorted -- the operating profit growth rate in the 30s is more reflective of META's underlying ability to grow its bottom line over time, I think.

Is META A Good Investment?

Meta Platforms combines many positives, I believe: It is the clear and undisputed leader in its industry, owning the top social networks and having the largest user count by far. Its business model allows the company to turn a mid-single-digit user count growth rate into a much larger revenue growth rate, thanks to its pricing power and due to META being able to show more and more ads to its users. And despite its heavy AI investments, META kept its margins at a highly compelling level.

Beating the consensus on both lines is great as well, of course -- which makes the post-earnings share price slump unreasonable, I believe. It is possible that some investors are unhappy about META's AI investments and heavy capital expenditures, but that isn't really new -- the company has been clear about its plans to ramp up its AI investments this year for some time.

Before the earnings release, META traded for around 22x net profits. When we account for the post-earnings share price decline and the earnings beat, which makes it likely that FY 2026 earnings will be higher than currently forecasted, the "true" earnings multiple for META is likely below 20 right now. For a company that is growing its revenue by more than 30% per year, that's a bargain valuation, I believe -- providing a nice chance to buy. META's shares often experience swings up and down -- the current slump to the $630 level, versus a 52-week high of around $800, makes META look highly compelling to me.

Markets can be irrational in the short run, providing opportunities for those who ignore the short-term noise: META's way-overdone selloff in 2022 and the huge recovery in the following years prove that.
